title: Developing the Spiritual Faculties
subtitle: Balancing Faith, Effort, and Awareness on the Path
epigraph: Those who are vigilant are in the presence of Nibbāna.
paragraphs: Yesterday we explored the hindrances—those states of mind that pull us away from our object. Even though they're called hindrances, they are our teachers, aren't they? They're there to show us what's wrong with us, and through our practice we can learn to deal with them skillfully. We have to look upon them as welcome enemies, something like that. We have to open our hearts to our hindrances.

Now there's the other side of the equation: the seven factors of enlightenment and the five spiritual faculties. I'm going to try and dovetail them, and by the end of this talk you'll be utterly confused. But I'll try to reduce it to a very simple statement at the end.

The best thing, especially for those who don't know what I'm talking about, is to let it go in one ear and out the other. This is more about giving you a certain confidence, a certain background knowledge that strengthens your faith in the process.

The ground of spiritual practice, in terms of advancement, is faith. I was going to say sīla, right conduct—of course we always talk about sīla and paññā. Paññā is the wisdom factor and sīla refers to conduct, and the one must be mirrored in the other. But here we're talking more specifically about developing this quality of intuitive insight.

Faith is crucial, and the first thing is not to confuse it with belief. If we define belief as faith in a statement—"I believe in a god" or "I don't believe in a god"—then that's not what the Buddha is talking about when he speaks of saddhā, faith. What he's talking about is a sense of confidence, a sense of trust.

If you think about it, anytime you act—especially at those moments in life when it's a big decision, such as moving house, taking a job elsewhere, forming a relationship, going to live with somebody, marrying them—all those occasions demand faith. You trust in your own decision. You trust in the way you see things, the way you understand things. There's something in the heart telling you that this is what you have to do; otherwise you just wouldn't do it. The relationship between trust and commitment is crucial.

If you don't trust, you just don't do it. If you go to a surgeon to have the pimple removed at the end of your nose and you don't feel right about him or her, you don't do it—you go and find somebody else.

Faith has two qualities: the quality of understanding and the quality of trust, which comes more from the heart. The first sort of understanding we get is simply from listening to Dharma talks, from reading. It's a given, and it makes sense.

The deeper understanding comes when that becomes our own way—when we've worked it out for ourselves. You might hear somebody explain the wheel of dependent origination, this psychology the Buddha taught, and it might initially make sense. But then when you reflect on it, it begins to make more sense. And you can see that with that deeper sense there grows a sense of trust coming from the heart, a willingness to investigate further.

Of course, through the practice of vipassanā, what was intellectual understanding becomes direct understanding, and that begins to cement the faith. It's said that faith doesn't become unshakeable until we've intuited nibbāna. Once a person has experienced nibbāna, there's no way that faith can be shaken, because now they know for themselves, absolutely, that the teaching of the Buddha is true. It's not a matter of losing faith anymore, and in fact the Buddha gives it a different word.

For one word it's called an indriya, a faculty. When somebody has an insight into nibbāna, it becomes a bala, a strength—unshakeable. The quality of that understanding, the process, is really just this deepening of understanding, and it has the effect of trust.

Now, that trust is coming from the heart, isn't it? It's something more intuitive. There are those people who need to understand the nth degree of the teaching before they dare to trust it. And there are those who don't need much understanding; they just trust the feeling they have towards a particular teaching or a particular teacher, and that's enough for them. You see that feeling, an intuitive feeling that this is right for them. Most of us, of course, have to balance the two in some proportion.

But you can understand that some people simply have faith. They just have it. You might diminish this by calling it blind faith because they can't draw up the arguments. But it can be just as deeply felt, just as deeply committed, as the faith of somebody who can argue their point.

It was often a criticism that somebody had blind faith. But if your blind faith is actually in the truth, that's good, isn't it? If it turns out that what you've got blind faith in is the absolute truth, it saves you a lot of reading for a start. You just get on with the practice.

This movement of the heart towards faith is there at the beginning of our practice, and it supports everything we're doing. There's a lovely image for it; I can't remember whether it came from the scriptures, but it's definitely in the Milindapañha.

The Milindapañha is a collection of questions and answers given by Nāgasena, who was understood to be fully awakened, some centuries after the Buddha when all the northern part of that world was Greek because Alexander had conquered it. Milinda is actually the Greek king Menander, who would have been around Afghanistan. And there Nāgasena talks about the water-purifying gem of the world-conquering monarch.

In the scriptures you get this myth of the world-conquering monarch. Those of you who know the story of the Buddha will remember that when he was seen by the wise man, the sage prophesied that this little baby would become either a world-conquering monarch or a fully self-enlightened being. That's what made his father so upset and determined to bring him up to be a world-conquering monarch. That's the story.

This alter ego runs right through the scriptures—that the Buddha could have become this, and if he had he would have been a superb leader of the world bringing tremendous good conduct and all that. One of the gems that such a person possesses, one of the things supporting that world-conquering manifestation, is this water-purifying gem.

When all the horses and camels and bullock carts have passed over the river with all the men carrying their shields and swords, of course the water is all disturbed. But by dropping this lovely gem into the water, the whole thing becomes utterly purified. That's a lovely image for faith.

Think about it: before you make a decision, there's confusion, isn't there? Shall I? Shall I not? There's lots of doubt about. You don't know whether you will or whether you won't. You make your lists for and against. And then suddenly you commit and the whole thing just disappears. Just like that. The heart with all its troubles and anxieties gets completely purified. It all vanishes and suddenly there's this lovely blossoming of joy in one's commitment. It's a lovely image to have for faith.

Now, that faith underpins paññā, this intuitive intelligence. It's because we have confidence in the teaching, confidence in the practice, that we can actually even start looking. And that looking, that observing, brings about its own insights, which then strengthen the faith. And so they tumble over each other into pure awakening.

This intuitive intelligence is expressed in another form among the seven factors of enlightenment: investigation of dharma, dhammavicaya. When the Buddha talks about that—if you read the discourse on it—it's basically beginning to look at things the way he saw things. We have those three characteristics: transience, not-self, and how we create unsatisfactions for ourselves. But also this business of deconstructing things, all this business around the hindrances, and the factors of enlightenment. That's all to do with investigating the Dharma, as he calls it.

That investigation is supported by equanimity. Equanimity is one of these funny words, really. It's not the same as calmness. I think equanimity is best described as a perfect receptivity. There's nothing stopping you from receiving. There's no reaction to what's actually coming at you.

If you liken it to a judge, we expect the judge not to get caught up in the clever little arguments of the lawyers—at the head level. And we don't expect them to get caught up in the anger and emotion of the crowd, baying for blood. It's that business of being able to be dispassionate, not caught up in things. And of course, as soon as we lift ourselves into the position of the observer, equanimity arises naturally—because to observe, you have to allow whatever is to be observed to manifest.

Supporting that equanimity, you've got concentration, which is balanced with effort, with proper energy. Concentration is another of those funny words, because normally when you say to somebody 'get concentrated' or 'be focused,' you expect this real narrowing down, a collapse into some tightness. But concentration in this sense just means being able to keep the attention on the object. Full stop.

And the effort to be developed is simply the effort needed to keep the attention there. If that concentration becomes tight, you start moving into restlessness. The attention begins to shake, and you can't keep your mind on it. When that happens, the meditator has to recognise it.

This is where the meditation gets, shall we say, a little tricky—because you may be thinking that the restlessness is coming from something arising out of the heart, but actually it might be because you're trying too hard. Trying to become too concentrated.

If you think of a camera when you focus it—the old ones, before they made these little machines, where you had to actually use your fingers—that turning of the lens is the effort, and then there's the focus. That's the relationship between effort and concentration.

Now the other side: when you have too much concentration but not enough energy, the mind is like a laser beam facing one way. And if the energy isn't there to support it, you drop into a state of unconsciousness.

That's not the same as sleep, and the meditator knows the difference, because the body remains upright—the energy is there alright, the concentration is there—but because the energy isn't supporting it, the mind just stops as a blank. You wake up, and depending on the power of that concentration, it can last for various lengths of time.

There's a lovely story about a Hindu guru who was an adept at these very high jhānic absorption states. He gave an instruction to his attendant to get him some water, then went to meditate and entered one of these states. Ten years later, he comes out and his first words are, 'Where's my water?'

What the tale is meant to symbolise is that nothing happens. It's a complete waste of time getting into these constricted blank states. And it's funny how many people actually believe that meditation is about blanking the mind, turning it off and ending up in this dead place.

When that happens, we can congratulate ourselves because the concentration is good, but we also have to recognise that something's wrong with the effort. Normally when you look at it, there's been a moment where you've lost interest, where something has lost its empowering of that looking, and the whole thing drops.

When the concentration has become too tight, you have to look at your effort—the effort to gain, the effort always to achieve. And when that happens, you have to really remind yourself that to be aware is the achievement. That's it. There's nothing else to achieve.

Once you drum that into yourself, it does stop you wanting to go beyond the present moment. Because whenever you want to achieve something, it's always in the future, isn't it? When you've achieved it, you've achieved it—but while you're wanting to achieve, it's not here, it's over there. The mind is always moving ahead of itself.

If you find yourself feeling restless, and you've got an inkling that it's not really to do with the body—because the body feels good, feels light, it's okay, but there's this restlessness—then, especially not so much at the beginning of a week retreat but maybe within two or three days when you really settle, just see if some idea of getting something has crept in.

There's a story from the scriptures about Nanda, the Buddha's cousin. Nanda was practising hard but getting nowhere—just restlessness and frustration. Eventually he began to give up. He decided that in this lifetime he would simply create good karma and have another go in the next one.

The Buddha, having heard this or intuited it, appeared by his side and said, 'Nanda, what are you doing, mate?' And he gave him a simile. He said, 'No, no, what you've got to do is have this effort'—and he gave the image of tuning a lute, a stringed instrument. To get the right pitch, it mustn't be too tight and it mustn't be too loose. Just getting the right pitch.

And of course, Nanda, having got the right pitch, became fully awakened in no length of time. Usually these little stories are glossed in the commentary to say things like 'Nanda went off to the forest for twenty-five years and became fully enlightened.' But actually the scriptures say he struggled with it and became fully awakened.

Effort is a cheeky one for us because Western culture is about achieving, about getting. And one of the problems of the Vipassanā school is that it does put certain achievements before us. They're there. They're called the vipassanā ñāṇas, the insights that come with vipassanā. And then there are four stages of sanctity: stream entrant, once returner, non-returner, and finally the arahant.

The way we can look at this so it doesn't become some sort of achievement is to recognise that the path is actually well delineated. It's not a path which is higgledy-piggledy and may or may not lead somewhere. The path leading to breakthrough is very clearly marked. Very clearly marked.

Having said that, the path of the vipassanā knowledge is not the only way. People have achieved amazing insight very quickly, usually because of some great shock. Somebody close has died, or they've fallen off a bridge or something. Something happens which gives them a shock and it can really pull them out of a particular mind state and turn their lives. One of the more interesting examples is the near-death experience.

So this business of effort and concentration, you see. Effort, by the way, comes up in the faculties too, for those of you who know these things. The other combination is calmness paired with what's badly translated as joy—much better translated as interest.

Now interest is a funny emotion. It's the philosopher's wonder. It's a wanting to know, but you can feel it coming from the heart. And just as an aside, ask yourself: did you ever have any problem with concentration when you were interested in something?

That's why, because the force of our practice is to awaken the intelligence, it seems to me much more beneficial to raise interest rather than to worry about concentration. And I'm always amazed how this manifests even in animals, this wonder.

I was once in a lovely place up in Penang, Malaya. There was a plantation up there and I was offered a stay at this house which was lived in only occasionally. It was a lovely evening, so I took a table out onto the forecourt, wanting to lift myself off the ground because of bugs and snakes.

I tied a mosquito net to the tree above me and brought my cushions out and a cup of coffee, and I sat on this table looking out through the mosquito net to the setting sun. As it grew darker and darker I suddenly realised I was facing east. I was a little disappointed.

There were these two dogs that every evening would scamper about, chasing each other—two very good friends who would chase each other madly around. On this particular evening, the one I was sat out there waiting for the sunset, one of these dogs was late. And the other one began to pine and cry a little.

When the dog finally turned up, to my amazement, he ran over—and when he saw this table with this figure under a mosquito net, he just stopped and stared. He stood there transfixed while the other dog was biting him and doing all sorts of daft things. I thought, my goodness, what an amazing intelligence. I must have looked really silly. To a dog, anyway.

So it's something we find in all creatures. It's a case of awakening that within us. And that wanting to know, that wonder—sometimes you have to work at it a bit, just to raise your energy towards interest. Like all these things, these are conditionings. So the more you condition yourself to become interested, the more interested you become.

And the more interested you become, the more you practise with verve and with right commitment. The more you practise with verve and right commitment, the more you see. And the more you see, the more you're delighted—so the more interested you become.

These things have an internal motor, and I would say that interest is a real motivation. But sometimes you have to feed it, especially when those little nasty things come up—like boredom, which is the direct enemy of interest. When that comes up, remember that boredom is what is to be observed, and don't be conned into thinking you need distraction.

There was a monk I knew who would go to his teacher and say, 'I've been watching this breath and I'm really bored.' The teacher said, 'Well, why don't you try watching it down here in the stomach?' So he went away, came back, and said, 'I'm watching it and I'm really bored.'

So the teacher said, 'Why don't you try a concentration exercise and look at discs?' There's an exercise of looking at coloured discs, which you use to gain concentration. He asked the monk what his favourite colour was—it might have been blue—and said, 'Well, get yourself a disc of blue and just say blue, blue.' That was it. The monk came back and said, 'Really boring.' So the teacher said, 'Well, try another colour,' you see.
This poor monk ended up depressed. He ended up in hospital. And when he finally came out of it, I think he understood the mistake—getting into boredom, trying to escape boredom through distraction.

You have to be very careful. If you look at our society, that's the problem with all this addiction stuff. It's all an escape from boredom, seeking pleasure in something which always has an inbuilt obsolescence. You can't keep chewing the same gobstopper, even if it is blackcurrant. You want a lime gobstopper. Eventually you can't stop eating gobstoppers, spend all your money on gobstoppers.

So remember that to raise interest is a very empowering way to put energy into our practice. On the other side, what supports it is calmness—calmness in the heart. And as I say, not to confuse that with equanimity. Calmness. Anything which you find is shaking, is shaking.

Of course, one of the things that happens when you draw up interest and begin to see something is that the excitement comes. And that shakes the meditation for us. So as soon as that comes, that's your object. Remember that the mind and heart are very slippery—they're always shifting off focus. So you have to keep that sort of aliveness.

And that, you won't be surprised to hear, is the establishment of awareness. What the Buddha says is that when you establish right awareness on the object, all these faculties will come up to support it, quite naturally.

The quality of awareness is just looking, just observing, just being with the experience. It's its simplicity which foxes us. We always think we have to do something. We always think we have to see something. You don't. All you have to do is look. It's extraordinarily simple.

In the opening of the discourse on how to establish right mindfulness—this awareness, satipaṭṭhāna, the establishment of this level of consciousness—the Buddha starts off by using the word sikkhati. Sikkhati means to train. So he says observe the breath to train. In other words, to get still, to be somewhat concentrated.

That phrase is in my morning vipassanā guidelines. And it's what one of my main teachers always used to say—that was his little phrase, 'somewhat concentrated.' Nobody ever knew exactly what he meant, but we got the idea that you needn't wait until you're utterly, completely focused before you could, as it were, begin to investigate.

Then the Buddha says—and he's talking now about the breath, which is something neutral, so the other two things are not so obvious, this business of not me, not mine, and the business of how we create problems from it—the most obvious thing to look at is the quality of transience. So he says keep looking at it. Now he's suggesting that you actually do that as a purposive act. Like the thought comes in the mind: let me watch the transient nature of this.

At the moment when I see the breath, it feels to me like one thing, like a string—like it's one string. How long is time? The length of time as we experience it is as long as we can hold a frame, a frame of time. So how long is it? Have you ever measured it? I don't know. Less than a second probably, or something like that. How long is a piece of time?

Now, that gives us the impression that the whole breath is one joined-up experience. What the Buddha is saying is: if you keep looking, if you keep just putting your eye on it, and the more these faculties come up to actually join you—and you're actually tuned into the transient nature of the breath, that's what you're looking at, is it just one string or is it all little pieces all quite separate—as you're looking at that, all these faculties rise up: concentration, right effort, investigation of the Dhamma (here it's the anicca quality), the balance of effort with concentration, equanimity balancing investigation, and calmness balancing interest.

When all these things rise up, the Buddha says you come to a point where there is just enough concentration and just enough intuitive intelligence for insight to arise. And those two words—awareness and intelligence—are never separate in that discourse.

The two words that I use for this trust of ours, satipaññā, I've actually taken from a very famous Thai teacher called Buddhadāsa. They're two nouns, but the way it's put in the scriptures is ātāpī sampajāno satimā. Ātāpī is the effort, and it's sometimes translated as fierce effort, but we're much closer to the meaning if we understand it as relentless effort. You don't stop. That's why I quote the Mahāsi: the continuity of awareness is the secret of success. It's the continuity, because the more you maintain it the more these spiritual faculties come up.

You begin by placing the sati on the object. That's the looking. And as you look, then you see. So the looking and seeing are both the same faculty—it's just that one is passive looking, receiving, and then there's the seeing.

If you think of any occasion—I had one where a friend came to see me and we were going for a walk, and his car broke down. We went off to have a look at it. The police had got there because he'd left it on the main road here in Newton Abbot and wheeled it back down. He opened the bonnet. What had happened was that suddenly the engine cut.

Because of my long experience with mechanics, I immediately said, 'Well, that's got to do with the electrics.' So he opened the bonnet and I immediately pointed to this object within the engine. Having looked, I saw it must be the dynamo and pointed to it. I said, 'The dynamo's gone.' The AA man turned up and, having a slightly longer experience in mechanics, noticed that the fan belt had gone and pointed to the dynamo—which happened to be in a different place.

So these two qualities—the sati, the awareness, and that spark, that intuitive grasp of what's happening—arise naturally when the conditions ripen. And in our meditation, when we're sat here, we're just making that continuous, relentless effort of just relaxing, of coming back onto the object.

That's another way, perhaps, of translating that word for calmness: passaddhi. You can translate it as relaxing. And then as it slowly gathers around, there comes the moment where we can hold the position of the observer. Now that's the point where we are seeing the transience, but there's still the sense of the observer.

The observer means there's a shaking in the mind. One minute we see the sensation of the breath; the next minute we're aware of something watching the sensation of the breath. And that awareness carries a feeling of me being here.

Here's a metaphor for what's really happening. Sometimes when you're watching TV you can see yourself reflected on the screen. Are you going to watch yourself watching TV, or are you going to watch the TV? Now, because of the light, you can't get rid of that image of yourself. But as your interest grows in the program, you don't see it anymore. You just get lost in the program itself.

When we're in a state of the observer, there's always that dual state going on: seeing, seeing the seer, seeing, seeing the seer. And of course, because of the speed of the mind and its ability to hold things long enough to substantiate them, give them some solidity, we feel it's one process—that there is an observer constantly observing this breath which is constantly arising and passing away.

But by continuously placing that attention—and that's another word for sati, an act of attention—on the object, all these faculties come up and the focus narrows and narrows. With it, time narrows too, until you're just there.

And when you're just there, there's no time for this switch, and the sense of self disappears. That's when it's possible for this intuitive intelligence to see something very clearly, because now it's not shaking. And there can't be any conceptual thinking in the now. It just can't be. Conceptual thinking takes time. You can't have a word in a speck of a second.

So when those moments come up there's always some little insight. They come in very short blips—so short you might not even know. But as your meditation grows, these little moments of being absolutely absorbed into the object grow slightly longer. And when you come out of that, back to being the observer, there is that knowing: during that moment there was pure sati-paññā.

Now do remember that you can't make any of this happen. That's the point. And that's where the sense of achieving comes in—so forget that. The Buddha is saying very clearly: just train, just keep bringing that attention back, keep waking it out of that dullness, just keep working with it gently. And there'll come that point when you'll begin to see these characteristics, one or the other.

As you begin to see that and your interest is drawn, you'll just be drawn naturally into the present moment. And that's when we have just enough awareness and just enough intuitive intelligence to make these little insights.

These insights are not huge wallops around the back of the head which send you spinning out of the room. They're just little—ah, oh, ah, right, see. And all the time this consciousness is turning, the way it's looking is changing, just in little, little degrees.

That then has a systemic change, because as we change our understanding it changes our relationship. So that's the heart coming in. And when a relationship changes it expresses itself through what we say and what we do and how we think—in a more wise way. That's the process we're going through.

If I were to bring it all together: there are these five spiritual faculties, and there are these seven factors of enlightenment. The ones to be concerned with are these. How to develop this awareness—just the looking, just the looking. How to support just the looking by raising interest. How to be very quick to know that some future aim has slipped into the practice—that's the achieving. How to be aware of that restlessness that comes around because we're trying too hard. And how sometimes you're right there but somehow you've slipped with the interest and you're sinking away from it.

So it's really a case of not worrying too much about all these different faculties and so on, but just keep reminding oneself to remain bright, vigilant. Now this word vigilance is one of the Buddha's favourites. Appamāda—vigilant, diligent, being awake.

In the vipassanā guidelines I've translated this phrase as 'those who are mindful are in the vicinity of, are in the presence of nibbāna.' The actual word the Buddha uses is appamāda—those who are vigilant, those who are diligent in their practice are in the vicinity and presence of nibbāna.
So it only remains for me to wish you fruitful practice and continuing effort. May you all be liberated even in this very lifetime. Thank you.
